author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

title: Of the Symbol of the Breast, and of the Shameful Mysteries of the Manichæans.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Lastly, there is the symbol of the breast, in which your very questionable chastity consists.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For though you do not forbid sexual intercourse, you, as the apostle long ago said, forbid marriage in the proper sense, although this is the only good excuse for such intercourse.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: No doubt you will exclaim against this, and will make it a reproach against us that you highly esteem and approve perfect chastity, but do not forbid marriage, because your followers—that is, those in the second grade among you—are allowed to have wives.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: After you have said this with great noise and heat, I will quietly ask, Is it not you who hold that begetting children, by which souls are confined in flesh, is a greater sin than cohabitation?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Is it not you who used to counsel us to observe as much as possible the time when a woman, after her purification, is most likely to conceive, and to abstain from cohabitation at that time, lest the soul should be entangled in flesh?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: This proves that you approve of having a wife, not for the procreation of children, but for the gratification of passion.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: In marriage, as the marriage law declares, the man and woman come together for the procreation of children.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore whoever makes the procreation of children a greater sin than copulation, forbids marriage, and makes the woman not a wife, but a mistress, who for some gifts presented to her is joined to the man to gratify his passion.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Where there is a wife there must be marriage.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But there is no marriage where motherhood is not in view; therefore neither is there a wife.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: In this way you forbid marriage.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Nor can you defend yourselves successfully from this charge, long ago brought against you prophetically by the Holy Spirit.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Moreover, when you are so eager in your desire to prevent the soul from being confined in flesh by conjugal intercourse, and so eager in asserting that the soul is set free from seed by the food of the saints, do you not sanction, unhappy beings, the suspicion entertained about you?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For why should it be true regarding corn and beans and lentils and other seeds, that when you eat them you wish to set free the soul, and not true of the seeds of animals?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For what you say of the flesh of a dead animal, that it is unclean because there is no soul in it, cannot be said of the seed of the animal; for you hold that it keeps confined the soul which will appear in the offspring, and you avow that the soul of Manichæus himself is thus confined.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: And as your followers cannot bring these seeds to you for purification, who will not suspect that you make this purification secretly among yourselves, and hide it from your followers, in case they should leave you?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: If you do not these things, as it is to be hoped you do not, still you see how open to suspicion your superstition is, and how impossible it is to blame men for thinking what your own profession suggests, when you maintain that you set free souls from bodies and from senses by eating and drinking.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: I wish to say no more about this: you see yourselves what room there is here for denunciation.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But as the matter is one rather to repress than to invite remark, and also as throughout my discourse my purpose appears of exaggerating nothing, and of keeping to bare facts and arguments, we shall pass on to other matters.
